methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series A → next-stage step-up multiplier (2.0x) to $30M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Space & Aerospace (found 0 same-stage, 2 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 11 same-sector peers exist in Space & Aerospace, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

methods.name: Public-Comp Anchoring
methods.skip_reason: target revenue not on file (private companies rarely disclose)
methods.rationale: Public Space & Aerospace P/S median is 25.8x across 6 tickers. Cannot apply without target's revenue.

data.narrative:
Atmos Space Cargo is a Series A-stage space and aerospace company founded by Sebastian Klaus, Marta Oliveira, Jeffrey Hendrikse, and Christian Grimm. The company has raised €30M to date. The broader Space & Aerospace sector comprises 6 public comparables (RKLB, PL, LUNR, ASTS, SATL) and 8 private peers including SpaceX, Sierra Space, Planet Labs, Impulse Space, and Apex Space.

Valuation analysis relies on stage-progression methodology, the only applicable triangulation approach given data constraints. Using total capital raised (€30M) as a proxy for current value and applying a 2.0x step-up multiplier typical of Series A-to-next-stage progression yields €60M. Public-comparable anchoring cannot be applied due to absence of target revenue disclosure, a standard limitation in private company valuation. Peer-median analysis was inapplicable; while 11 same-sector peers exist, only 2 adjacent-stage comparables were identified, falling short of the minimum 3-point threshold required for statistical reliability.

The analysis supports a valuation range of €51M (low) to €69M (high), centered at €60M. This range reflects the stage-progression method and encompasses reasonable variance around the point estimate. Key uncertainties include the absence of revenue or financial metrics for comparative anchoring and limited visibility into same-stage peer transactions in the frontier-space sector. A more granular valuation would require either disclosed revenue figures or access to recent Series A transaction data in space cargo specifically.
